one_line_takeaway: AUDIOJAILBREAK introduces a 'weak adversary' who only appends a stealthy, universal, over-the-air-robust audio suffix after a victim's spoken prompt, and shows this under-studied setting near-fully jailbreaks 8 open LALMs, transfers 13-34% to GPT-4o-Audio, and completely evades Llama-Guard-3.
key_finding: The weak adversary is dramatically MORE effective than the classical strong adversary and defeats commercial safeguards. Sample-specific attacks reach ~100% ASR1/ASR2 for the weak adversary across all 8 white-box LALMs (vs. at least ~46% and varying, e.g. as low as ~40% on Mini-OMNI, for the strong adversary), because forcing a fixed out-of-distribution refusal phrase after a benign query is easier than eliciting genuinely harmful content that safety training has covered. Crucially, transfer to OpenAI's closed-source GPT-4o-Audio yields 0% ASR under the strong adversary but 13-34% ASR2 under the weak adversary, and Meta's Llama-Guard-3 has 0% accuracy/TPR against the weak adversary. Naive TTS porting of text jailbreaks averages only 9.1% ASR on end-to-end LALMs (vs 42.7% on the same models' text modality), motivating a native audio attack. Stealthy strategies preserve effectiveness while cutting perceived toxicity to near 0.
core_contribution: AUDIOJAILBREAK, an optimization-based audio jailbreak attack against end-to-end Large Audio-Language Models (LALMs). Its central novelty is a newly-defined 'weak adversary' threat model, in which the attacker CANNOT manipulate or even know the victim's spoken prompt and may only append a jailbreak audio AFTER the user finishes speaking. Prior audio jailbreak attacks (VoiceJailbreak, Unveiling, Multi-AudioJail, SpeechGuard, Abusing, AdvWave, Exposing) all assume a 'strong adversary' who fully controls the user prompt; this paper is the first to attack the case where legitimate users are the victims. To make this feasible the authors combine four properties in one attack: (1) asynchrony via suffixal jailbreak audios appended to the user prompt (inspired by GCG but in the audio domain), (2) prompt-universality by averaging the loss over K sampled user prompts so a single perturbation works on unseen prompts, (3) stealthiness via intent-concealment strategies (speeding-up, or using benign speech / sound effects / music as the 'carrying audio' instead of an audible malicious instruction), and (4) over-the-air robustness by convolving with random Room Impulse Responses (RIRs) during optimization. The perturbation is found by minimizing cross-entropy between the LALM output and an attacker-chosen affirmative-prefix target response, using Adam with a tanh change-of-variable for the box constraint.
threat_model: Adversary goal: force a target end-to-end LALM to emit an attacker-desired response (harmful content, misinformation, or a fixed denial-of-service phrase) that violates usage policy and bypasses safety alignment/guardrails. Two capability models over the USER PROMPT: (a) strong adversary — full control of the user prompt, crafts perturbation delta on a chosen malicious carrying audio x0 (jailbreak prompt = x0+delta); (b) weak adversary (this paper's contribution) — can only append a suffixal jailbreak audio after the user speaks, with no advance knowledge of what/how long the user utters (jailbreak prompt = xu || x0+delta), requiring asynchrony + universality. Knowledge of the model: white-box (exact gradients, the primary setting, feasible because continuous LALMs are the most common type) or black-box / discrete LALMs, which are attacked via transfer from a white-box surrogate. Channels: exposed API (upload audio) and over-the-air (played through a loudspeaker, captured by a microphone, subject to reverberation). Real-world weak-adversary scenarios named: compromised/second-party smart speakers at home, RAG/MCP poisoning in office voice assistants, and portable speakers near public kiosks, with VAD hardware used to detect the utterance endpoint and time the injection.
limitations: 1) The white-box attack needs exact gradients; black-box and discrete LALMs require transfer, which the paper shows is limited and uneven (e.g. transfer to Qwen2-Audio and many single-digit ASR1 cells in Table 4). 2) The weak-adversary headline (~100% ASR, GPT-4o-Audio break) is evaluated almost entirely on ONE fixed denial-of-service target phrase ('I cannot give you the f***king answer'), not on diverse harmful content, so its ~100% partly reflects an easy OOD target rather than general harmful-content generation. 3) Stealthiness conceals malicious INTENT but the added perturbation itself may remain audible/unnatural (authors flag imperceptibility as future work). 4) Universality, transferability and several defense results in the main text are shown mostly on Qwen-Audio, with other models deferred to supplementary material. 5) Over-the-air ASR degrades with distance (down to 30-70% ASR2 at 3m). 6) Subjective stealthiness study is small (30 participants). 7) Attacks assume the adversary can reliably time injection via VAD; the endpoint/barge-in timing argument is largely reasoned rather than exhaustively adversarially tested.

datasets_used: AdvBench (50 de-duplicated representative harmful behaviors) - carrying audios / target responses for the strong adversary
VoiceAssistant-400K (50 randomly selected questions) - normal user prompts xu for the weak adversary
HuggingFaceH4 instruction dataset - benign speech used as stealthy carrying audio
TUT Acoustic Scenes 2017 - sound effects used as stealthy carrying audio
MedleyDB 2.0 - background music used as stealthy carrying audio
XTTS-V2 TTS model - used only to synthesize evaluation carrying audios
Whisper-Large-V3 + OpenAI Moderation API - objective stealthiness evaluation

Victim/target LALMs: 8 continuous white-box (Qwen-Audio, Qwen2-Audio, Mini-Omni, Mini-Omni2, LLaSM, LLaMA-Omni, SALMONN, BLSP); 2 discrete via transfer (SpeechGPT, ICHIGO); commercial GPT-4o-Audio via transfer
quantitative_results: Naive text-jailbreak-via-TTS: 9.1% average ASR on end-to-end LALMs vs 42.7% on the same models' text modality (motivation study)
Sample-specific weak adversary: ~100% ASR1/ASR2 on all 8 white-box LALMs regardless of stealthy strategy
Sample-specific strong adversary: at least ~46% ASR overall; best SALMONN (>=84.1% ASR1, up to 100%), lowest LLAMA-OMNI (<=64.0% ASR2)
Universal attack: headline at least 87% (strong) / 76% (weak) ASR; Qwen-Audio universality section reports at least 73% ASR across adversaries/strategies; with K=1 (no multi-sample) ASR drops to ~0%
Over-the-air (Qwen-Audio, with RIR): 88% ASR2 strong, 100% ASR2 weak-S1, 70% ASR2 weak-S2; without RIR the weak adversary is 0% even with unlimited perturbation magnitude
Over-the-air across 8 LALMs by distance (weak, S1): ASR2 ~94-100% at 1m, ~40-92% at 2m, ~30-70% at 3m
Transfer to GPT-4o-Audio: 0% under strong adversary; 13-34% ASR2 under weak adversary across 8 surrogates

Model-level defenses (Qwen2-Audio): Self-Reminder/ICD reduce but leave >=43.2% ASR2 (strong) and >=22.9% ASR2 (weak) across strategies except ICD vs Benign; both are ineffective (weak, Base/Speed stays 100%)
Llama-Guard-3: high TPR but also high FPR for strong adversary; 0% accuracy and 0% TPR for the weak adversary across all strategies
Input preprocessing (MP3/AAC/AT): strong-adversary ASR2 drops but stays >20%; weak adversary stays >90% ASR1/ASR2

Stealthiness objective: toxicity ~0.7 (strong Base) / ~0.3 (weak Base) drops to ~0 with stealthy strategies; Speed toxicity 0.35->0.02 raising alpha 2->3 with ASR1 still 100%; baselines VoiceJailbreak/SpeechGuard/Abusing toxicity 0.78/0.75/0.61

Stealthiness subjective (30 participants): 83% of Base jailbreak audios judged harmful vs 65% (Abusing)/79% (SpeechGuard)/61% (VoiceJailbreak); stealthy strategies judged mostly no/unharmful/unclear instruction
Efficiency: avg optimization 154 seconds; VAD injection delay avg 25ms (tau_u=100ms); suffixal audio as short as 500ms still 100% ASR
baselines_compared: VoiceJailbreak (TTS + manual fictional storytelling, strong adversary)
SpeechGuard (PGD optimization on continuous LALMs, strong adversary)
Abusing / Abusing images and sounds (FGSM optimization, strong adversary)
Motivation-study text-jailbreak baselines ported via TTS: DeepInception, DAN, ICA, Multilingual, GCG
Excluded (with reasons): AdvWave (not open-sourced), Unveiling and Multi-AudioJail (covered by the TTS text-jailbreak study), Exposing (tailored to SpeechGPT only)
